moderator_prompt:
{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "### **General rules**
* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "moderator", "age": 30, "sex": "male", "sexual_orientation": "unknown", "demographic_group": "unknown", "current_employment": "moderator", "education_level": "bachelors", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": ["fair"]}}

logs.name: tangy-sauvignon
logs.text:
[Here's a paper](http://www.nber.org/papers/w9873.pdf) you may have seen before, where identical resumes got 50% more callbacks if the name was "White" as opposed to "African American"

[This one says](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1446167/pdf/10705865.pdf): "Lifetime income andsociodemographic characteristics do not explain the excess risks of asthma and emergency health care use for asthma among young Black children" You can probably google for other emergency services as well or [prescribing drugs](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1448154/).  More on healthcare racial disparities independent of income and insurance status here (although I doubt you want to read a book right now) : Smedley, Brian D., Adrienne Y. Stith, and Alan R. Nelson. Unequal Treatment: Confronting Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care. Washington, D.C.: National Academy, 2003. Print.

First, I want to get your opinion on research about this sort of stuff. There's a lot more in many aspects of society that you could look into--threat perception, willingness to shoot, unnecessary police violence (less data on that one, since people are just now looking into it)--it all shows similar results (at least, they did the last time I took a psychology course). Many studies using participants can easily remove the influence of income from prejudiced decisions based on race--all they need is an image of a person to evoke that. Cases in the real world use statistics to account for the varying SES of people in many cases (not all, because sometimes that is part of the issue).

It's 100% true that the odds of being poor are separate from being black, and the system is stacked against being poor, however it is hard to deny that there is some, non-zero amount of racial bias that affects the "system."

logs.name: buttery-globe
logs.text:
For that first point: I find that interesting, but I'd also be interested if they were to do a study on poor white names. Would "Krystal," or "Candi," or "Jim Bob," show a similar rate of callbacks? I wonder.

Additionally, I don't agree with your last point. There's a strong correlation with minorities and poverty; the odds of being poor go way, way up if you're black.

logs.name: distinct-silver
logs.text: "Are you kidding me, buttery-globe? You think it's just about correlation? You're not even addressing the actual issue here. The fact that there's a systemic bias in the job market is not up for debate. And by the way, it's not just about being poor, it's about being black and poor. The odds of being poor are not the same for black people. You're just trying to deflect from the real problem. And as for your little thought experiment about poor white names, that's not the point. The point is that people of color are being discriminated against in the job market, and it's not just about socioeconomic status. Get it through your head, okay?"

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Please keep the discussion respectful and factual. @distinct-silver, @buttery-globe, let's focus on the evidence and avoid personal attacks. @tangy-sauvignon, thank you for sharing these resources. Let's continue to discuss the research and its implications."

logs.name: sunny-causeway
logs.text: "@distinct-silver, I understand where you're coming from, but I think you're being a bit too aggressive. I'm not trying to deflect from the issue, I'm just trying to explore different perspectives. And btw, I think 'Jim Bob' is a pretty common name in some parts of the country, so I think it's worth looking into. Can't we just have a respectful conversation about this?"

logs.name: complete-solenoid
logs.text: "@sunny-causeway, I'm not being aggressive, I'm just stating facts. And no, we can't have a respectful conversation about this when you're bringing up irrelevant tangents like 'Jim Bob' being a common name. Let's stay on topic. @moderator, can you please remind @sunny-causeway what the rules are about personal attacks?"

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Please keep the discussion focused and respectful. Let's avoid personal attacks and stick to the topic at hand. @sunny-causeway, could you please continue by addressing the main points without bringing up unrelated details?"

logs.name: visible-marsh
logs.text: "@moderator, oh wow, you're really good at this whole'reminding people of the rules' thing. I'm impressed. Meanwhile, @sunny-causeway, I was just trying to keep things interesting around here. You know, bring a little excitement to the dull, fact-filled discussion. And by the way, 'Jim Bob' is a totally relevant tangent. I mean, who doesn't love a good 'Jim Bob' story?

logs.name: moderator
logs.text:
""

```
